pattern: Fixing a starting position in each string and walking both forward together lets a single running mismatch counter tell you, at every length, whether the two substrings starting there differ by exactly one character.
hints: Instead of generating every substring pair from scratch, fix a start index i in the first string and a start index j in the second string, then extend both together one character at a time.

As you extend from (i, j), keep a running count of mismatched character positions seen so far; the moment it exceeds one, stop extending that pair -- any longer substring from the same start would only have more mismatches.
Every time the running mismatch count is exactly one (after extending by at least one character), the current pair of substrings is a valid match -- add one to the answer and keep going.

edge_inputs.description: both strings the shortest possible, single character, no match
both strings the shortest possible, single character, exactly one diff
identical strings, so no substring pair differs by exactly one character at length 1 but longer aligned pairs can
completely disjoint alphabets, zero matches possible
repeated characters causing many overlapping candidate alignments

stress.description: random lowercase strings over a small alphabet to create many partial matches
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, min(size, 60))
    m = max(1, min(size, 60))
    alphabet = string.ascii_lowercase[:4]
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))
    t = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(m))
    return [s, t]
